does: This paper introduces CUSP, a benchmark for evaluating whether AI systems can forecast scientific progress across multiple disciplines. The framework uses temporally grounded evaluation with knowledge cutoffs to test frontier models on 4,760 scientific events, assessing their ability to evaluate research feasibility, reason about mechanisms, design solutions, and predict timing of advances. The authors find that current models can identify plausible research directions among alternatives but systematically fail to predict whether scientific advances will actually occur and consistently misestimate when they will happen. Performance varies substantially by domain, with AI-related progress more predictable than advances in biology, chemistry, and physics, and is largely unaffected by model scale or fine-tuning approaches tested.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining AI's role in scientific discovery and the emerging interest in using language models for scientific reasoning and forecasting. It extends prior work on benchmarking scientific knowledge and reasoning by introducing temporal grounding and event-level evaluation, responding to questions about whether systems like those evaluated in prior scientific NLP tasks can actually forecast novel developments. The paper connects to research on prediction markets and subjective probability assessment in science, while also relating to studies of AI capabilities across domains that have shown heterogeneous performance. It engages with broader efforts to evaluate whether large language models possess genuine predictive understanding versus pattern matching, particularly for out-of-distribution scenarios.
enables: This benchmark and analysis enable several future directions. The identified gap between identifying plausible directions and predicting realization suggests investigation into what additional information or reasoning processes models need to improve forecasting accuracy. The domain-dependent performance patterns point toward understanding whether certain scientific fields have more inherent predictability or whether models capture some fields' literature better. The work opens investigations into whether incorporating explicit uncertainty quantification, ensemble approaches, or hybrid human-AI methods could improve scientific forecasting. It also enables studying how models might be trained or architected to better capture temporal dynamics and practical constraints in science, potentially informing efforts to use AI for actual scientific priority-setting and resource allocation.
